Shrine buildings |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}{{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}} 1-14-13 |Coordinates = {{Coord|38|18|1.4|N|140|59|53.0|E|scale:20000_region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Taga Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = April 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Taga Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taga Shrine''' (Taga Jinja) is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}, located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokugatsuzaka|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}} is also one of the disputed shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsya|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Taga Shrine” of {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taga Shrine''' (Taga Jinja) is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}, located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokugatsuzaka|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}} is also one of the disputed shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsya|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Taga Shrine” of {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taga Shrine''' (Taga Jinja) is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}, located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokugatsuzaka|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}} is also one of the disputed shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsya|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Taga Shrine” of {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

1-14-13 |Coordinates = {{Coord|38|18|1.4|N|140|59|53.0|E|scale:20000_region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Taga Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = |Main hall style = Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taga Shrine''' (Taga Jinja) is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}, located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokugatsuzaka|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}} is also one of the disputed shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsya|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Taga Shrine” of {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

category|Category:Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}} [[JA:多賀神社 (多賀城市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11431090}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Taga Shrine (Takasaki) |Image = Shrine buildings |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}{{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}} 1-14-13 |Coordinates = {{Coord|38|18|1.4|N|140|59|53.0|E|scale:20000_region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Taga Shrine}} |Deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = April 15 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Taga Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taga Shrine''' (Taga Jinja) is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}, located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokugatsuzaka|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}} is also one of the disputed shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsya|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Taga Shrine” of {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taga Shrine''' (Taga Jinja) is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}, located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokugatsuzaka|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}} is also one of the disputed shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsya|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Taga Shrine” of {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}

Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taga Shrine''' (Taga Jinja) is an {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}, located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokugatsuzaka|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Takasaki (Tagajō City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|lt=Tagajō City|Q653355|WD=Q653355|1=Tagajō}}, {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|WD=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}. {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}} is also one of the disputed shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsya|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}) for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} “Taga Shrine” of {{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|WD=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}. === History === The shrine was established by transfer (kanjō) from {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|WD=Q843882|1=Ōmi Province}}. In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 15 ({{ill|lt=796|Q33452|WD=Q33452|1=796}}), it received the divine rank of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|1=UNKNOWN}} from the {{ill|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. Later, as {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} declined, the shrine also fell into decline and its location became unknown. At Shiogama Shrine, the Ichinomiya of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, the 1745 record “Shiogama Shaki” ({{ill|lt=Enkyō|Q1183728|WD=Q1183728|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} 2) speculated that the foundation stones at “Tō-no-koshihara,” where the pagoda ruins of the former Tagajō temple now stand, were the remains of the ancient grand shrine “Taga Shrine.” In the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=An’ei|1=UNKNOWN}} 3 [{{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|WD=Q7697|1=1774}}]) submitted by order of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|lt=Ukishima Shrine (Tagajō City)|Q11558528|WD=Q11558528|lt=Ukishima Shrine|1=Ukishima Shrine}}, the village tutelary shrine of Ukishima Village, was identified as “Taga Shrine.” It states that Taga Shrine of Ōmi Province was enshrined there, and that the shrine’s vertical plaque clearly bore the four characters “Taga Myōjin.” The village tutelary shrine “Shinmei-sha,” located in the hamlet “Tō-no-koshi” of Takasaki Village, is described in the “Fudoki Goyōshodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774], Takasaki Village) as having an unknown origin of enshrinement, with no mention of any connection to “Taga Shrine.” Instead, for “Tō-no-koshihara,” it records a tradition that “there once stood a seven-hall temple complex with a five-story pagoda,” and notes that “even now the stone foundations remain exposed.” The hamlet names for Shinmei-sha and the temple ruin foundation stones differ, and although their locations are adjacent, they appear to have been slightly offset. From the late {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period onward, this Shinmei-sha came to be called “Taga Shrine.”In Meiji 21 ({{ill|lt=1888|Q7829|WD=Q7829|1=1888}}), the 1:20,000 rapid survey map “Shiogama” prepared by the Army Land Survey Department of the Second Division General Staff labeled the shrine at Rokugatsuzaka as “Taga-byō” and the shrine at Takasaki as “Shinmei-byō.” After the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 8 (1919) promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|lt=Law for the Preservation of Historic Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments|Q10916213|WD=Q10916213|1=Historical Sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments Preservation Law}}, the temple ruins were designated as a historic site in Taishō 11 (1921) as “associated temple ruins” together with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagajō ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}. At that time, the site was referred to as the “Takasaki Temple Ruins,” based on the former village name. The principal deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|WD=Q2963653|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, and {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} are also enshrined. The shrine is located southeast of {{ill|lt=Tagajō|Q870867|WD=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}}, which served as the provincial capital ({{ill|lt=kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088|1=Kokufu}}) of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, adjacent to the Tagajō temple ruins. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Taga Shrine, Miyagi District, Mutsu Province,” and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its former rank was village shrine. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}) September, it was officially designated a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Subsequently, the Shinmei Shrine and Tenman Shrine in Tomegaya, and the Shibiki Shrine in Higashi-Tanaka, were {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} into it. The shrine buildings once stood on the pagoda foundation platform of the Tagajō temple ruins, but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40 ({{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}}), when the Tagajō temple ruins were designated a national {{ill|lt=Special Historic Site|Q26764449|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}}, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its current location. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}).According to an explanatory sign erected beside the shrine by the shrine parish representatives. At the Taga Shrine in Rokugatsuzaka, located almost directly north of the main hall of the Tagajō government complex, many bamboo hoops (taga) woven from bamboo—like those dedicated at the head shrine {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}}, which enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanagi|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izanami|1=UNKNOWN}}—have been offered.According to Tōshirō Shimizu, “Tagajō Ruins,” in *Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Report*, Vol. 3, Miyagi Prefecture Historic Sites, Scenic Places, and Natural Monuments Survey Committee, Shōwa 2 (1927), pp. 41–134, in the section “Legends of Various Places within Tagajō” (Kikuchi Mitsuya, pp. 128–131), it states: “Taga Shrine lies north of the inner fortress, beside the old Shiogama road. Villagers string together bamboo hoops equal in number to their age and hang them before the shrine to pray for longevity. Behind this small shrine, the foundation stones of the former shrine buildings are scattered, and several large stones excavated nearby are all massive. It is said that the former shrine buildings were grand and magnificent. The fields behind the shrine are said to be the sites of ancient halls, and even now they are cultivated without fertilization. In a corner of the temple ruins at Takasaki, on the pagoda platform, there is a Taga Shrine, but it was moved from here; before this shrine there are no hoop offerings, and villagers commonly refer to it as ‘Oise-sama.’” At present, the Tagajō City Board of Education officially recognizes this as the “Engishiki Shrine: Taga Shrine,” and has installed official markers and information boards. The former shrine site lies about 50 meters northeast of the current location, and through excavation surveys and environmental improvement projects in the Rokugatsuzaka area, the shrine was {{ill|lt=relocated|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}} to its present site. Before relocation, a {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} gate was also in place, but it no longer exists. In the “An’ei Fudoki Shodashi” (An’ei 3 [1774]) of Ichikawa Village, only four shrines—the village tutelary Sōsha Myōjin Shrine (present-day {{ill|lt=Mutsu Sōsha Shrine|Q11658605|WD=Q11658605|1=Mutsu Sōsha-no-miya}}), Arahaba-ki Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, and Hachiman Shrine—are listed; this shrine is not mentioned, and details of its shrine traditions remain unknown. === Access === 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Kokufu-Tagajō Station|Q898924|WD=Q898924|1=Kokufu-Tagajō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Tohoku Main Line|Q681190|WD=Q681190|1=Tōhoku Main Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Commonscat|Taga-jinja (Tagajo)}}{{Shinto horizontal}}
